excerpt: O world, deceive someone else! I have divorced you thrice, after which there is no return.
source_location_hint: Hikam (Sayings), the address to the dunya — 'Yā dunyā ghurrī ghayrī'

excerpt: The virtues of the Commander of the Faithful are without number; whoever loves him, his faith is complete.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya, opening chapters on the merits of ʿAli

excerpt: The light by which the prophets prophesied and the awliyaʾ were guided is the light of the Wasi.
source_location_hint: Section on the rank of the Wasi and his relation to the Natiq

excerpt: ʿAli is from me and I am from ʿAli; he is the wasi after me, and the imam of the believers.
source_location_hint: Kitab al-Walaya, the chapter on the designation (naṣṣ) of ʿAli

excerpt: I am the one whom God has given knowledge of what was and what shall be; ask me before you lose me.
source_location_hint: Khutab (Sermons), the Kufan sermons — 'Saluni qabla an tafqiduni'

excerpt: Al-Kawthar is the abundance of progeny through Fatima, and ʿAli is the channel through which this abundance is preserved.
source_location_hint: Esoteric interpretation of the short Suras; section on Surat al-Kawthar

excerpt: The straight path mentioned in the Qurʾān is read by the people of taʾwīl as the path of ʿAlī, the Imām being himself the ṣirāṭ along which the believer travels to the Originator.
source_authority: Fatimid chief qāḍī, foundational Ismaili jurist and esoterist
source_location_hint: Discussion of the ṣirāṭ mustaqīm as the Imām

excerpt: Q 33:23 is read in Ismaili taʾwīl as a portrait of those believers, foremost ʿAlī, who held their covenant with God without alteration.
source_authority: Fatimid chief qāḍī, foundational Ismaili jurist and esoterist
source_location_hint: Taʾwīl of Q 33:23 within treatment of covenant verses

excerpt: The Light Verse (Q 24:35) is interpreted as a parable of the daʿwa: the niche is the Prophet, the lamp within it the waṣī ʿAlī, and 'light upon light' the unbroken succession of the imāms.
source_authority: Major Iranian Ismaili philosopher of the 4th/10th century
source_location_hint: Yanbūʿ on the Light Verse and the hierarchy of the daʿwa

excerpt: At Ghadīr Khumm the Prophet declared, 'Of whomever I am the master (mawlā), ʿAlī is also his master,' establishing the waṣiyya by which the imāmate passed to him.
source_authority: Fatimid chief qāḍī, foundational Ismaili jurist
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya, opening section on the imāmate of ʿAlī

excerpt: The ḥadīth in which the Prophet declares to ʿAlī: 'You are to me as Hārūn was to Mūsā, save that there shall be no prophet after me' — adduced as canonical proof of ʿAlī's status as waṣī, parallel to Yūshaʿ ibn Nūn vis-à-vis Mūsā.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya, vol. 1 — section on the merits and the testamentary designation of ʿAlī

excerpt: The Ismaili doctrine that the whole of creation subsists in pairs of ẓāhir and bāṭin, active and passive, each member of the pair completing its companion — a foundational principle of Fatimid cosmology.
source_location_hint: on the pairing (izdiwāj) within the cosmic order

excerpt: The Prophetic ḥadīth: 'I am the city of knowledge and ʿAlī is its gate' (Anā madīnat al-ʿilm wa-ʿAlī bābuhā), adduced in support of the waṣī's role as the sole licit gateway to prophetic knowledge.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Walāya, on the merits of ʿAlī

excerpt: The seven-tier hierarchy of natural kinds — mineral, plant, insect, cattle, beast of prey, bird, human — in which humankind is the comprehensive seventh rank, used as a cosmological parallel for the wilāya of the waṣī completing the six pillars.
source_location_hint: on the hierarchy of natural kinds and the place of the human

excerpt: On the day of Ghadīr Khumm the Prophet declared: 'Of whomsoever I am the mawlā, ʿAlī is his mawlā.'
source_authority: Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān b. Muḥammad al-Tamīmī (d. 363/974), chief jurist of the Fatimid imamate
source_location_hint: Book of Walāya, on the event of Ghadīr Khumm

excerpt: The Imams from the progeny of al-Ḥusayn are those whom God chose from the offspring of His Messenger and appointed as guides summoning humankind to His path.
source_authority: Jaʿfar b. Manṣūr al-Yaman (fl. 4th/10th c.), early Fatimid dāʿī and authority on imamate doctrine
source_location_hint: On the line of Imams from al-Ḥusayn as God-chosen guides

excerpt: I am leaving among you two weighty things: the Book of God and my household; whoever holds fast to them shall not go astray. They are as the Ark of Nūḥ — whoever boards it is saved, and whoever lags behind is drowned.
source_authority: Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān b. Muḥammad al-Tamīmī (d. 363/974), chief jurist of the Fatimid imamate
source_location_hint: Book of Walāya, ḥadīth al-thaqalayn with the simile of the Ark

excerpt: al-Ḥasan and al-Ḥusayn are the masters of the youth of the people of Paradise.
source_authority: Qāḍī al-Nuʿmān b. Muḥammad al-Tamīmī (d. 363/974), chief jurist of the Fatimid imamate
source_location_hint: Book of Walāya, on the virtues of al-Ḥasan and al-Ḥusayn

excerpt: The verse of purification descends upon five: the Messenger, ʿAlī, Fāṭima, al-Ḥasan, and al-Ḥusayn. By it God has set them apart from every defilement, and so He has set them apart in rank.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-walāya, on the merits of the Ahl al-Bayt

excerpt: The family of Yāsīn are the progeny of Muḥammad, for he is Yāsīn; and God's greeting upon them is a greeting upon the Imāms from his house, generation after generation.
source_location_hint: On the taʾwīl of Sūrat al-Ṣāffāt and the salām upon Āl Yāsīn

excerpt: The Messenger said to ʿAlī: 'You are to me in the station of Hārūn to Mūsā, except that there is no prophet after me.' By this he made him his brother, his minister, and his successor.
source_location_hint: On the prophetic mission of Mūsā and its taʾwīl in relation to the waṣiyya of ʿAlī

excerpt: As Hārūn was the wazīr of Mūsā in his daʿwa, so ʿAlī is the wazīr of Muḥammad in his daʿwa; and the sunna of God runs through His messengers and their waṣīs without alteration.
source_location_hint: On the parallel of nubuwwa and waṣiyya across the cycles

excerpt: The election of the family of Ibrāhīm and the family of ʿImrān is the figure of the election of the family of Muḥammad; for the later cycle inherits and surpasses the chosen-ness of the cycles before it.
source_location_hint: On the chosen-ness (iṣṭifāʾ) of the prophetic houses

excerpt: The Prophet Muḥammad is the source of ʿAlī's complete instruction; ʿAlī is the bāb (gate) through which the inner knowledge of the Messenger is transmitted to the community.
source_location_hint: Vol. 1, Kitāb al-Walāya, on the transmission of Prophetic knowledge to ʿAlī

excerpt: The Prophet declared that ʿAlī is with the Qurʾān and the Qurʾān is with ʿAlī, the two never separating until they return to him at the pool of Kawthar — the hadith of the two weighty things (al-thaqalayn) underpinning the pairing of the Book and the walī as joint witnesses to prophethood.
source_location_hint: Vol. 1, Kitāb al-Walāya, opening sections on the indispensability of pairing the Book and the Imam

excerpt: Knowledge (ʿilm) and action (ʿamal) are inseparable in the daʿwa; the ẓāhir of practice without the bāṭin of knowledge is empty, and knowledge without practice is barren.
source_authority: Fatimid bāb al-abwāb (chief dāʿī) under al-Mustanṣir (d. 470/1078)
source_location_hint: Discourses on the necessary pairing of ʿilm and ʿamal in the path of the imams

excerpt: The wilāya of the Imam is the protective covenant ʿahd that shields the believer from the corrosive doctrines of the extremists and the negators alike; without it, the natural sciences cannot lead to knowledge of the Truth.
source_location_hint: Vol. 1, Kitāb al-Walāya, on the imam's walāya as the firmest handhold (al-ʿurwa al-wuthqā)

excerpt: In the Tayyibī cosmology of cycles (adwār), each cycle of seventy is presided over by a foremost figure of its age; the Qurʾān is the scripture of the present cycle, revealed in honour of the foremost personage of that cycle.
source_authority: Fifth Ṭayyibī dāʿī muṭlaq of Yemen (d. 612/1215)
source_location_hint: Sections on cycles of revelation and hierarchical ranks of the natiqs and asas

excerpt: ʿAlī's epithets — slayer of the nākithīn and qāsiṭīn, guardian of the Prophet, ark of knowledge, imam of the pious — recur as the standard Shīʿī taxonomy of his spiritual offices.
source_location_hint: Sections on ʿAlī's spiritual ranks, his role as waṣī, and his function as the bāṭin counterpart of the Prophet

excerpt: Generosity (sakhāwa) as the outward sign of inward freedom from attachment, a station of the awliyāʾ.
source_location_hint: Kitāb al-Zakāt; sections on the ethics of giving

excerpt: The hadith 'You are to me as Hārūn was to Mūsā' (ḥadīth al-manzila) is a central proof-text for the walāya of ʿAlī in Ismaili literature.
source_location_hint: chapters establishing the designation (naṣṣ) of ʿAlī

excerpt: The multiplicity of names of the Imam corresponds to his manifold relations to the ranks of being and the worlds of disclosure.
source_authority: chief dāʿī of the Fatimid daʿwa under al-Mustanṣir
source_location_hint: majālis on the stations and titles of the Imam

excerpt: Salmān al-Fārisī as one who attained recognition of the inner reality of the Imam; the saying 'Salmān minnā ahl al-bayt' is widely transmitted.
source_location_hint: chapters on the virtues (faḍāʾil) of the Companions of ʿAlī

excerpt: Discernment (tamyīz) between the rightful Imam and his counterfeit belongs only to those endowed with inner sight.
source_authority: chief dāʿī of the Fatimid daʿwa under al-Mustanṣir
source_location_hint: majālis on recognition (maʿrifa) of the Imam

excerpt: The world of bodies is the shadow of an inward world; allegiance to the friend of God conditions the very dispositions of creatures.
source_location_hint: yanābīʿ on the correspondence between the sensible and the intelligible worlds
